turns.text: Hello Darlene, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you. It is… it is a lot to carry, you know? Being twenty-six, alone, walking through the winter here in Worcester, I feel like I am carrying the whole world in my stomach. Everything is new. Every time I feel that little *thump*—that little movement—I get so happy, but then, a second later, I am scared. 

I know the books. I know how the brain grows, how the cells build the... the connection, the nervous system. But in the clinic, it is hard. My doctor, she looks at my papers, and she says "everything is good, Darlene, you are low risk." And I want to say, "But wait, are you sure about the little folds in the brain? Is it growing fast enough?" 

But the words... they just go away. My mouth gets dry. I have the feelings, I have the worry in my heart, but in English, I feel like a child. I just nod. I say "fine" because that is the only word that comes out when I am nervous. I feel like they see "healthy patient," but they don't see that I am a mother who is just very, very tired of being afraid in silence. I am glad you are listening. It’s the first time someone asked me what I feel, not just how my blood pressure is.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

It is hard to say there is a "relationship" when the clock is always ticking. My doctor, she is nice, I think. She has a kind face, but she is always in a hurry. She comes in, she checks the belly, she listens for the heart—*thump-thump, thump-thump*—and then she is writing on the paper. 

I want to ask her, "When the baby hears my voice, does it change the way the brain wires itself?" But then I think, "How do I say 'neuro-pathways' or 'synapse' when I am still looking for the word for 'stomach ache'?" So, the relationship feels… thin. Like paper. It is all about the "low risk" box she checks. 

There was one nurse, though, a few weeks ago. She didn't just rush. She saw me looking at the ultrasound screen, trying to understand the grey shapes, and she just put her hand on my shoulder. She didn't ask me a question, she just waited. I didn't say anything, but I didn't feel like I had to be "fine" for her. That felt important. Mostly, though, I feel like I am just a patient number. I am not Darlene to them; I am just a pregnancy that is going the way it is supposed to. I wish they knew that just because I am quiet, it doesn't mean I am not drowning in questions.
Who else was involved in your care that you'd like to mention — community midwives, consultants, support workers, interpreters?

That is the thing—there is nobody else. It is just me. I don’t have a midwife, and I haven't been offered any support workers or someone to help me talk. Sometimes, when the appointment is coming up, I think about asking for someone to help with the language, but then I worry. If I bring someone with me, will they tell the doctor the things I am too scared to say? Or will they just make it more confusing?

I feel very alone in this. My family is far away, and I am working so much, trying to save money for the baby, so I don't really have the time to find a group or a community. I am just... navigating the map by myself. I think, sometimes, that if I had someone—maybe another woman who understands both the medicine and the feeling of being a stranger in this country—I wouldn't stay up all night staring at the ceiling. 

I see other women in the waiting room with their partners or their mothers, and they look so relaxed. They ask questions—long ones—and the doctor sits down to answer. I just sit with my backpack and my worry, waiting for my turn to be "low risk" for ten minutes. It is a very quiet way to become a mother.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

It is just me. I am doing this on my own. There is no partner to hold my hand in the exam room, and honestly, sometimes I think that makes the silence feel even bigger. When you are alone, the doctor doesn't have to explain anything to a "team"—they just talk to me, and if I don't catch the word, the moment is just... gone. 

Day-to-day, the absence is the hardest part. I come home from work, and the apartment is so still. I put my hand on my belly and I talk to the baby in my own language, because that’s the only time I feel like I have a voice that is strong and sure. I tell the baby all the things I want to ask the doctor. I tell him about how the world is going to look and how I am going to make sure he is smart and safe. 

But then, the next morning, I have to go back into the world where I am the "quiet, polite girl." It’s a strange feeling, being someone who is so capable in my head but so small in the clinic. If I had someone with me—a friend, or even just someone who knew my language—I think I could be brave enough to stop the doctor and say, "Wait, explain this to me." Without that, I just carry it all inside. I am the protector, the researcher, and the nervous mother all at once, and there is no one to share the weight of it with.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

I think there is a misunderstanding. When I say I am doing this alone, I really mean it. There is no partner to have a relationship with—not anymore. It is just me, my work, and the baby growing inside me. So, there is no tension between me and a partner, because there is only me.

But, if you mean, does this bring me "closer" to the baby? Yes, in a way that is hard to explain. Because I am the only one who feels these movements, and because I am the only one who worries about the little brain folds, it makes the bond between me and my child very deep. It is like we are a secret team. I am the only one who knows what he needs, even if I don't always have the words to tell the doctors.

It is a lonely kind of closeness, though. Sometimes I look in the mirror and I don't recognize the woman looking back. I look tired. I look like I’m hiding a lot. I wonder if the baby knows that I am trying my best, even when I am standing in the clinic shaking because I can’t explain that I am worried about his development. I hope he knows I am fighting for him, even if I am fighting in silence.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

I don’t really have anyone here. Back home, things are different—you talk to your aunts, your sisters, your mother; there is always someone who has walked the path before you. But here? My friends are mostly colleagues from work, and they are busy with their own lives. We talk about shifts, about the rent, about the weather—not about the delicate, terrifying way a brain forms. I think if I started talking about the ventricles or the cortex, they would look at me like I am crazy. I don't want to be the "worried pregnant girl."

I tried looking online once, in my own language, but the medical websites... they are so heavy. They talk about tragedies and complications, and I get so scared I have to close the laptop. It’s like, I look for comfort but I only find more things to fear.

So, I don't really have a support bridge between me and the clinic. The formal care is one island, and my life is another, and there is no boat between them. I just keep the questions inside until they start to feel like lead. I think the doctors think I am a "good patient" because I don't cause trouble, but really, I am just a person who feels like she is screaming underwater, hoping someone will notice the bubbles.
Who else was involved that perhaps you didn't expect to lean on — an online group, a work colleague, a community figure?

There isn't really anyone. That’s the most honest answer I can give. I think, when I first found out, I had this romantic idea that I would find a community here—maybe a group of other mothers who were also working and navigating this new life. But reality is just... hard. 

When you spend your whole day working to afford the rent in Worcester, and you’re trying to learn the rules of a medical system that wasn’t built for you, you don’t have much energy left for people. I keep to myself at work because, honestly, I am scared that if I tell them I am struggling, or that I am scared about the baby, they might see me as... weak? Or "high risk"? I need this job. I cannot risk anyone thinking I am not capable.

So, I suppose the only person I’ve "leaned on" is the baby. That sounds strange, I know. But when I’m on the bus, or when I’m lying in the dark, I talk to him. I tell him, "We are doing this. We are learning the words together." It’s a very solitary kind of leaning. It’s like I’m propping myself up against my own shadow. I didn't expect to be this isolated, but it is the truth of my life right now.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

The biggest decision so far was the anatomy scan coming up. For many women, it’s just a routine date on the calendar, but for me, it feels like the moment of truth. My doctor—she gave me the paper for it and just said, "Standard screening." She didn't explain what it meant if they found a "soft marker" or something that looked slightly different. She just handed me the paper like it was a grocery list.

I didn't have anyone to talk it over with. I sat at my kitchen table, the paper in front of me, looking up words on my phone. *Ventriculomegaly*. *Cerebellum*. I was trying to understand what the scan would actually show versus what it wouldn't. I wanted to ask, "If you see something that isn't quite right, what is the next step? Do we look at the brain structure deeper?" 

But in that office, the decision was only mine, and I felt paralyzed. I kept thinking, "If I say yes to all these tests, am I just borrowing trouble?" I wanted to say no, I wanted to say yes, I wanted to say "help me understand." But I just signed the paper. I felt like I was signing away my peace of mind. It was a lonely kind of surrender. I had to decide to trust a process that I don't fully understand, with a language that feels like a wall, all by myself. It’s hard to make choices when you feel like you're playing a game where someone else holds all the cards.
How did that affect your sense of being in control of your own care — did you feel like the decision was truly yours?

Control? I don't think I have ever felt in control since I stepped into that clinic. It feels more like I am a passenger in a car driving very fast, and I am sitting in the back seat, not even able to see the map. 

When I signed that paper for the anatomy scan, I didn't feel like I was making an informed choice as a mother. I felt like I was just following instructions. If I were to tell the doctor, "I am not sure I want this, can you explain the risks and the benefits again, slowly?"—I am terrified she would look at me with that tired, rushed expression and think I am just being difficult or "emotional." 

The system treats me like a box to be checked. They ask, "Any concerns?" and when I say "No," because I cannot find the words to express the depth of my fears, the box gets checked, and they move on. That is not my choice; that is a system moving around me. 

I want to be the one steering. I want to be the one who knows what is happening inside my own body. But instead, I feel like I am just an object they are observing. It makes me feel very small, and sometimes, very angry. Is this how it’s supposed to feel? Like I am an observer of my own pregnancy?

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

My job is the biggest pressure. My employer, they are... they are business people. They see a pregnant belly and I think they see a liability, even if they don't say it. I keep my head down and work twice as hard as everyone else because I am so terrified of being the first one they let go. I haven't even told them the full details of my appointments. I just say, "I have a medical thing," and I rush back as soon as I can. The constant worry that I might lose my income—that is a weight that sits right on top of the baby. It makes my heart race, and I worry that the baby feels my stress, too.

As for my community, there is a distance. I live in a world where everyone is busy, and I am the only one carrying this secret. There isn't a faith community I go to here; I think I stopped looking for one because I didn't want to explain why I am doing this on my own. It’s easier to just be the "quiet, hard-working Darlene." 

Sometimes, I go to the park on my day off, just to be near people. I see families laughing, and I think, "Will I ever be part of a picture like that?" It’s a very strange life, knowing so much about the development of a human being while knowing so little about how to find a community that will hold me while I do it. I feel like I am invisible, honestly. I am just a worker, just a patient, just a body in the system. I don't feel like a *person* to anyone except my child.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

When I am in that small, cold room, it is usually just the doctor and me. Sometimes, a medical student will stand in the corner with a notebook. That makes it worse. It makes me feel like a lesson, a case study to be observed rather than a woman expecting her first child. 

When they touch my belly—the cold gel, the pressure of the probe—I feel like my body doesn't belong to me. They talk *over* me, using words that sound sharp and clinical. Even if I know the definitions, the way they throw them around makes me feel small, like I am just an anatomy textbook with a heartbeat. The doctor will say, "Fundal height is within range," and I want to ask what that feels like for the baby, if he has enough room to turn, but the way she looks at her clipboard makes it clear that my curiosity is just an inconvenience.

If a nurse is there, I sometimes feel a little bit more human. If she catches my eye or smiles, I feel like I am back in my own body again. But when it is just the doctor and the student, I feel like I am just a container. It is a lonely, cold way to experience the most intimate time of my life. I find myself holding my breath, just waiting for the door to open so I can finally leave and be "Darlene" again, not just a set of clinical measurements.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

Oh, every time. Every single time. When I am there alone, I feel so… exposed. It is not just about the medical exam—it is the vulnerability of having to be both the patient and the translator for my own heart. If I had a friend or someone who knew me, they could hold my hand, yes, but they could also bridge that gap. They could ask the question I am holding in my throat. When I am alone, there is no buffer between me and the clinical coldness.

I remember once, the doctor was looking at the scan, and she just sighed. She didn't say anything, she just sighed and went back to writing. My heart stopped. I thought, "Is something wrong? Did the heart rhythm change? Are the brain ventricles too big?" I wanted to scream, to ask her why she sighed. But I couldn’t. I just sat there, shaking inside, feeling like I was waiting for a verdict on a life I love more than my own. 

If someone else had been there, they would have seen that sigh and demanded an answer. They would have made it a *conversation*. Being alone means I have to swallow my fear because I am too busy trying to be "the good, quiet girl" so they don't look at me differently. It makes the exam room feel like a place where I am losing a little bit of myself every visit.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

There was nobody to help. When I look back, I realize I navigated everything by sheer, stubborn force. I am the one who spends hours translating websites on my phone in the middle of the night. I am the one who figures out the bus schedule to get to the clinic across town. 

The system itself makes it hard. It’s like a maze where the walls keep shifting. When I called to make my first appointment, the person on the phone spoke so fast, and when I asked them to repeat, they just sounded impatient. I felt so small, like I was already failing at being a mother because I didn't know the "right" way to schedule a check-up.

No one told me about my rights. No one gave me a guide that said, "Darlene, you can bring a translator," or "You can ask for a different doctor if this one doesn't explain things to you." It feels like the system is designed for people who already have all the answers. If you don't know the right questions to ask, you just don't get the information.

My work makes it harder, too. Every time I need to change an appointment because the clinic schedule doesn't match my shift, I feel like I'm balancing on a tightrope. No one is on my side. It’s just me, trying to catch every ball before it falls, terrified that one day I’ll miss one and everything will come crashing down. I wish someone—just one person—had sat me down at the start and said, "Here is how this works, and here is how we can help you." But that person never appeared.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

When I first realized, I think I was looking for a "mother figure" in the clinic, you know? Someone who would say, "Darlene, you are doing a good job, and here is what is going to happen." I wanted to trust my doctor because of the white coat—that is what we are taught, right? That they are the experts.

But trust is a two-way street, and it needs a shared language to build. I wanted to trust her because she has the credentials, but trust also comes from feeling *seen*. When I walk into her office, she looks at the chart, not at my face. She doesn’t ask, "How are you handling the stress of the city?" or "Do you have anyone here to help you?" If she asked those things, I would trust her with my life.

Instead, I feel like I am just a task she has to complete before her lunch break. There is no warmth, no "soft" space for me to be anything other than a record of health statistics. I don't feel like I can rely on her for my *fears*, only for the measurements of the baby's growth. I am still looking for that person—someone who would look at my chart, then look at *me*, and actually *see* the woman behind the "low risk" label. That is who I would trust. But for now, that person is just a ghost I haven't met.
Were there any moments where that trust felt shaken, or where you felt your concerns weren't being taken seriously?

Every single visit, I think. There was this one time—the anatomy scan—where I felt so much. The technician was moving the probe, and she kept stopping, looking at the screen, and then tapping the keys. I could see the image: the little brain, the tiny structures. I wanted to ask, "Is that normal? Are those the ventricles?" 

But she didn't look at me. She just said, "Hold still."

When I asked if everything looked okay, she just gave a tight little smile and said, "The doctor will discuss the results with you later." That felt like a wall being slammed in my face. My heart was pounding so hard I thought it would shake the table. I felt like they were hiding something, or maybe they just didn't think I was smart enough to understand the answer. 

I felt so small in that moment. My concerns weren't just ignored—they were silenced. It made me realize that to them, I am not a partner in this pregnancy. I am just a task to be managed. When you feel like your questions don't matter, you start to feel like *you* don't matter, either. That’s when the trust breaks. It isn't a big explosion; it’s just this quiet, cold realization that I am in this alone, and the people who are supposed to be helping are just ticking boxes on a screen.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

Before I was pregnant, I didn’t have a doctor here. I was working, paying my rent, and just trying to survive. I didn't have the luxury of a "primary care provider" to talk about health or planning. I was just focused on the day-to-day. 

But looking back, I think I was naive. I thought the system was a safety net, something that caught you when you fell. Now, I see it more like a gate—a gate that opens if you speak the right language and know the right people, but stays shut if you are just a girl from another country, working hard and trying to be invisible.

When I finally entered the clinic, the first few visits felt like they were just telling me who I was. "You are low risk," they said. They didn't ask about my family history, or my stress, or what I knew about the baby. They just put a label on me. It was like I was walking into a story that was already written, and I was just a character who had to play her part without ever seeing the script. 

I’ve had to make so many decisions about my own life, but in that clinic, I feel like I'm just following arrows on the floor. It feels like the decisions were made for me by a manual I’ve never been allowed to read.
Can you tell me a bit more about that moment? Who else was in the room, and how did it feel?

It was just me and the doctor, the one with the brisk, efficient way of speaking. She was reviewing my initial intake paperwork. I remember her tapping her pen against the clipboard, her eyes scanning the lines like she was checking off a grocery list. 

"You're twenty-six, no history, good blood pressure, low risk," she said, not looking at me. She didn't ask how I felt about the pregnancy or how I was handling the stress of work or being on my own. It felt like she was reading a description of a stranger, not me. I wanted to tell her, "Yes, I am low risk on paper, but I am terrified inside because I don't know what to expect," but the words just felt like they were stuck in my throat.

I remember sitting there, feeling very small in that bright, sterile room, with the hum of the computer in the background. It felt like she was deciding who I was—just a "low risk" patient. There was no room for me to be anything else. When she stood up and moved toward the door, signaling that the session was over, I felt like a door had been closed on me. I didn't feel like a person; I felt like a case that had been successfully sorted. It was cold. It was lonely. It was a reminder that in this system, my feelings are not part of the plan.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

I think I used to see my body as a machine—it worked, it got me to work, it paid the bills, it did what it was told. I never really thought about it as something that could create life, you know? It was just... me. I was always so focused on my brain, on reading, on learning how things work. I didn't spend time thinking about the biology of *me*.

When I got pregnant, that all changed. Suddenly, my body was not mine anymore. It belonged to the baby, and it belonged to the clinic. I don't talk about it with anyone. Who would I talk to? My friends back home, they have their own lives, and I don't want them to worry about me being here by myself. 

I’ve always been a private person. I grew up believing that if you have a problem, you fix it yourself. You don't complain, you just work harder. So, talking about the changes—the morning sickness, the tiredness, the way my belly feels like it’s stretching into something new—it feels almost like admitting I am failing. I keep it all inside. I look at other women, how they hold their bellies, how they talk about their plans, and I feel like I am watching a movie in a language I don't quite understand. It’s a very private, quiet transformation. It’s beautiful, but it’s also very, very lonely.
Were there parts of your relationship with your body that felt complicated or uncertain as you thought about becoming pregnant?

It’s… complicated. Even before this, I always felt like my body was something I had to control, to keep moving, to keep working. When I thought about being pregnant, I think I was afraid of losing that control. I’m twenty-six, I’m working full-time—if I get sick, if I get too tired, who takes care of me? I worried that my body would let me down, or that I wouldn't know how to listen to the signals it was sending.

And there is a part of me that feels very strange about the medical side of it. My body is doing this incredible thing—making a brain, making a heart—but in the clinic, it’s just a "case." They measure, they poke, they look at the screen. Sometimes I feel like I am fighting a war against the medical, sterile way they see me. I want to tell them, "This is not just a patient, this is my home."

It feels like a betrayal, in a way. I’ve always been so proud of being independent, of being smart. But now, I feel like my body is doing things I don't fully understand, and I’m relying on people who don't see me as a person to tell me if I’m "okay." It’s a very weird, heavy feeling, like I’m a stranger living in my own skin. Does that make sense?
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

It is a jungle online, truly. When I started looking, I was like a hunter—looking for something that felt... solid. At first, I went to these big, famous websites, the ones that end in ".gov" or are run by big hospitals. I thought, "These must be the truth." They have the charts, the diagrams of the fetus, the lists of what to eat. They are *safe*. They don't make you panic, but they are also so cold. They speak in a way that feels like it was written by a machine for a machine.

But then, I found myself scrolling through forums, looking for something that sounded like a *human voice*. I would look for posts from other women who felt lost or who were doing this alone. I looked for people who used words that sounded like my own struggles. If someone wrote, "I was scared today at my scan because I didn't know what the doctor meant," I felt like, "Yes! That is me." 

I had to learn to balance the two. I use the "official" sites to learn the names of the parts of the brain—so I can pretend to know the words when I’m in the clinic—and I use the forums to feel a little less like I’m going crazy. I don't trust everything, though. Anything that tries to sell me a supplement or promises a "perfect pregnancy" based on some secret trick? I delete those. I want the truth, even if it is just a lonely, difficult truth. I’m just trying to build my own map because nobody gave me one.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

I think about my age—twenty-six—and I think, "Am I too young to do this?" I worry that because I am young and alone, they see me as less than, like a mistake rather than a mother. Every time I walk into that clinic, I feel like I have to wear my "serious" face to prove that I am not just a young girl who doesn't know what she’s doing. 

I’m also very thin. I’ve always been that way, even with the baby growing. I see the nurses look at the charts, look at me, and look at the chart again. They haven't said anything—yet—but I can see them wondering if I am eating enough, if I am strong enough. It makes me feel like my body is being judged, like it's not quite "right" for the part it’s supposed to play. 

It makes me feel like I’m constantly on trial. I’m not just growing a life; I’m trying to convince them that my body is capable of doing it. It’s like I have to perform being a "perfect, healthy mother" so they don't start adding extra tests or extra worries. I want them to see the strength in me, but I’m afraid all they see is a young woman who is alone. That feeling of being watched, and judged, it just adds to the silence.
Did anyone ever make you feel judged or reduced to a health risk rather than seen as a whole person?

Yes. It happens in the small ways. There was a time when I had to fill out a long form about my living situation—my income, my address, my marital status. I felt the person behind the desk look at the "Never Married" box, then look at me. She didn't say anything mean, but the way she clicked her pen and sighed… it made me feel like I was a problem to be solved, not a mother to be supported.

It’s like they see the "risk" before they see the woman. They see my background, my age, the fact that I am by myself, and they put me in a box that says "potential complication." I am not a complication; I am a person who is working very hard to give this baby the best start, even when the ground beneath me feels like it’s shifting. 

When they reduce me to a risk, they take away my voice. They stop asking me what I think or how I feel, and they start telling me what I *should* do, as if I haven't been doing the research myself. I want them to look at my medical charts, yes, but I also want them to see the woman who stays up all night reading about fetal neurology. I am not just a set of statistics; I am a mother. But in that clinic, I am just a risk factor with a pulse. It makes me want to scream, but I just stay quiet. I have to.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

I have to be honest—this is a part of my story that is very painful to touch. Before this pregnancy, there was no "we." There was no partner sitting at a kitchen table talking about the future or planning for a baby. The whole idea of becoming a mother was something I held entirely in my own hands, like a flickering candle in the dark. 

I never had those conversations where two people sit down and weigh the pros and cons, or talk about health, or think about what kind of parents they want to be. I didn't have someone to go to a doctor with, or someone to help me decode the medical jargon before I even stepped through the doors of the clinic. 

It makes the isolation feel so much sharper now. When I see other women with their partners, I don’t just see support; I see someone who acts as a witness to their life. I’ve had to be my own witness. Every decision—about my health, about my body, about the way I am going to bring this child into the world—has been made in total, absolute silence. It makes me feel like I’m walking through a storm, and I’m the only one holding the umbrella. I don’t have any memories of shared excitement or shared fear; I only have my own.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

I think there is a misunderstanding. I have said this before—I am alone in this. There is no partner, no father, no one. I go to every appointment by myself. Every time I walk through those heavy clinic doors, I am the only one there for me and the baby.

When you ask how a partner changes the dynamic, I have to imagine it. I watch the other women in the waiting room. I see their partners holding their bags, asking the questions they are too shy to ask, or just holding their hands when the doctor talks about something difficult. It looks like a partnership. It looks like they are a team. 

For me, the dynamic is just... me and the cold room. I am the one who has to be the translator, the researcher, the worried mother, and the "good patient" all at once. There is no one to witness the tiny, miraculous moments of the scan, and there is no one to hold my hand when I feel like my questions are being dismissed. It is just me, sitting on that paper-covered table, feeling like I have to be strong enough for both of us. It is a heavy, quiet existence. I am used to it, but that doesn't mean it doesn't ache.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

Before the pregnancy, I hardly interacted with the system. I was healthy, so I stayed away. But looking back at the rare times I had to go in for a quick check-up, I remember a specific moment when I felt like a human being. I had an issue with a bad cough, and a nurse actually looked me in the eye. She didn't look at the computer. She asked me, "Darlene, what do you think is causing it?" She wanted my opinion on my own body. That made me feel like my voice was a tool, not an annoyance. I felt like a partner in my care.

But then, I remember another time, different clinic, where the doctor just wrote a prescription before I even finished explaining my symptoms. I felt like I was just a slot in a machine. My words felt like static—just noise to be filtered out until they could get to the diagnosis and move on to the next person.

Now that I’m pregnant, those two feelings are in constant war. I am so desperate for the nurse who listens, but I keep getting the doctor who is rushing to the finish line. It makes me feel like I have to "perform" being a good patient just to be heard. It is exhausting to have to earn the right to have a voice when you are already carrying so much.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

At the beginning, I think I was just hoping to survive the appointments without crying or feeling stupid. I wanted someone to just tell me everything was okay, and I was willing to be quiet to get that reassurance. I wanted to be "the good patient."

But as the weeks go by, and I feel him moving inside me—that little secret code—my patience for being silent is running out. Now, I find myself wanting to be a *participant*, not a spectator. I don't just want to be told, "The baby is fine." I want to know *why* they think that. I want to be the one who asks about the ventricles and the cortex, and I want to be treated like someone who has the right to know the details of her own body. 

It’s becoming a bit of a battle, though. Every time I try to push for more information, I feel the doctor’s wall go up. It makes me realize that if I want to be in charge, I have to stop being the "polite girl." I have to be the woman who demands space. It’s scary, because I’m so alone, but I’m starting to realize that if I don’t advocate for myself and my child, no one else is going to do it for me. I’m tired of being a passenger. I want the steering wheel.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

It is rare, but there was one moment. A technician, not the doctor, actually stopped the screen and asked, "What does it feel like when he moves?" She didn't look at the metrics or the growth charts for a second. She just looked at me, really looked at me, with a soft smile. In that moment, I felt like the expert on *my* experience, because I was the only one who knew the rhythm of his life inside me. I told her, "It’s like he’s playing, but sometimes he’s quiet for so long I worry." She nodded and said, "That’s normal, you know him better than anyone." It made me feel so… validated. I wasn't just a patient; I was a mother who knew her child.

But most of the time? I am not the expert; I am the observer. The doctors treat me like I am reading from a script they wrote. When I try to share a concern—about how heavy the winter air feels, or the constant, gnawing worry about his development—it’s like they hear, but they don't *listen*. They deflect. They say, "Everything is low risk, Darlene." It feels like they are saying, "Stop worrying, you’re just a girl, leave the brain science to us." 

That is when I feel the least respected. They don't see the hours I spend researching, the way I track every movement. They just see the "low risk" box. I want to be the expert on my child's life, but the system keeps trying to make me the student. It hurts, because I know that I am the one who will be with him when we leave this clinic, and I want to be the one who knows everything.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

I need to be very clear, because I think it is hard for people to understand: there is nobody. There is no partner. There is no mother, no sister, no friend in this country who knows what I am going through. Every single decision—from the vitamins I take to the doctor I chose—has been made in the quiet, crowded space of my own mind. 

When I look at the birth plan, which is just another piece of paper the clinic gave me, I feel this ache in my chest. Other women have someone to discuss "pain management" or "delivery preferences" with. They have someone to hold the other side of the paper. I have to look at these options and imagine the birth alone. It makes the decisions feel heavy—like I am building a house by myself, brick by brick, and I am terrified that I might put one in the wrong place.

It is not just that I *want* help; it is that I feel I *need* a witness. Someone to say, "Yes, this makes sense," or "Maybe we should ask the doctor about this." Without that, I am just guessing. I am strong, and I am a mother, but sometimes I think it is too much for one person to carry all of this alone. The absence of someone else makes the entire journey feel like a long, dark tunnel where I am the only one holding a light.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

I feel like I am repeating myself, and it is frustrating, but I need to be understood: there is nobody. I am not married. I am not seeing anyone. I go through those sliding doors completely by myself. Every time.

So, when you ask how a partner changes what is said, I cannot tell you. I only know the silence of a room where it is just me and the doctor. I know the feeling of a doctor who speaks faster because she assumes I don't need a pause for a second opinion or a translator to explain the medical, scary words. If I had someone with me—a partner, a friend, a mother—maybe the doctor would slow down. Maybe she would explain the "anatomy scan" results in words that aren't just for a textbook. 

When you are alone, you are a target for being ignored. The doctor doesn't have to explain anything to a partner, so she just brushes past me. She doesn't have to look at anyone else, so she avoids my eyes. I have to be the one to speak up, but I am so scared of being labeled "difficult" that I just fold my hands and listen. It’s like being in a conversation where only one person is allowed to talk, and the other person—me—is just a piece of the furniture. It makes the whole experience feel like a test I am failing, even when I am doing my absolute best.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

It is a strange, double-edged feeling. In one way, I feel closer to my body than ever—I am the only one who knows when he is awake, when he stretches, or when he hiccups. It is a secret world. But the other side of it is that I feel like a stranger in my own skin. My body is doing things—swelling, stretching, changing—that I don't feel I have permission to celebrate because I am so busy just trying to keep my life on track. 

There isn’t a single soul I feel safe talking to about this. If I told my colleagues, they’d think I’m complaining. If I told the doctor, she’d probably just write "anxious" in my file and move on to the next appointment. So, I keep it all inside. I look in the mirror and I see the changes, and sometimes I feel a flash of awe, but then the fear creeps back in. I wonder if my body is "right" enough, if it’s "healthy" enough, if it’s "strong" enough to carry him through the end. 

I’ve had to build a wall around my feelings just to keep functioning. I’ve become very good at being "fine," but that mask makes me feel even more disconnected from myself. I feel like I am guarding a beautiful secret, but I’m the only one who knows it’s there, and that makes the silence feel like it’s ringing in my ears.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

There was one moment—it was small, but it stuck to me like a burr. I was at my appointment, and the doctor was measuring my belly. She looked at the tape, then at me, and made this little clicking sound with her tongue. She didn't say anything bad, really, just, "You're measuring small for this stage." 

That was it. That was all. But that one word—*small*—it felt like a judgment. I went home and I sat on the floor, looking at my belly, wondering, "Is he not growing enough? Did I not eat the right thing? Is my body failing him?" 

Because I am by myself, there was no one to tell me, "Don't worry, every baby is different." There was no one to hold my hand and say, "You are doing fine." Instead, I spent the whole night in a panic, trying to find information on fetal growth charts, terrified that my body was not enough. 

It feels like my body is being audited, and I am the one being found lacking. When they talk about my body like it's just a set of measurements, they forget that I am the one living inside it. They forget that I feel every stretch, every kick, every moment of fear. I am not just a "small" belly; I am a mother who is trying her hardest to make a safe home for her baby. It hurts to be treated like I am failing when I am just trying to survive.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think… I think I just want people to know that "low risk" is not the same thing as "no fear." I sit in that clinic and I feel like if I am not "high risk" on their charts, my worries don't have a place in their office. But I have so many questions. I worry about the brain development, the way the synapses form, the way the world I’m carrying him in is so cold and so fast. 

I want to tell them: stop looking at the clipboard and look at *me*. Even if I don't have the perfect English words, I have a heart, and I have a mind that is full of this child. When they ignore my questions or rush through the scan, they aren't just ignoring a patient—they are ignoring a mother who is trying to be the best protector she can be in a country that feels like it doesn't quite see her.

I’m doing this alone, and that is hard, but it’s the silence that is the heaviest part. I just wish there was someone—anyone—who would sit down, look me in the eye, and say, "Darlene, what is it that makes you stay awake at night?" If they did that, I think I could finally breathe. Thank you for listening. It feels like the first time I have been allowed to speak the truth of it.
